prediction: The DHT22 humidity sensor (GPIO 14 on ESP32) will show a measurable upward drift in humidity readings over the next 7 days (from 96.1% toward 99%+), because DHT22 sensors drift upward over time in stable-temperature environments. If the humidity is genuinely high (not sensor error), it will remain between 90-98%.
rationale: DHT22 sensors are known to drift upward in humidity readings over time, especially in stable-temperature environments where the sensor has no drying cycle. The 96.1% reading is at the high end of normal; sustained high humidity indoors without AC is unusual. If drift is confirmed, it would be a useful calibration signal for the embodied cognition system.

prediction: Applying the same Kalman filter parameters (Q=0.01, R=1.0) that were optimized for humidity to the DHT22 temperature sensor will show <50% variance reduction, because temperature noise characteristics differ from humidity noise. Temperature readings will require recalibration of Q/R parameters.
rationale: The Kalman filter Q/R parameters (process_noise=0.01, measurement_noise=1.0) were tuned for humidity data showing 0.8% oscillation amplitude. Temperature has different physical noise characteristics — DHT22 temperature precision is +-0.5C vs humidity +-2-5%. The same Q/R ratio may not be optimal. If variance reduction exceeds 50%, the parameters transfer well and the filter is more general-purpose than I assumed.

prediction: The DHT22 humidity sensor will either (a) stabilize between 96-97% over the next 5 days (confirming the initial reading was anomalous), or (b) continue drifting toward 99% (confirming the C524 prediction).
rationale: DHT22 sensors have ±2-5% measurement uncertainty. A 0.8% change could be sensor noise, not actual drift. The next 5 days will reveal whether this is genuine drift or measurement noise.
